title: Precision: Being Right When You Say Yes
concept: Precision measures how many of the model's positive predictions were actually correct, minimizing annoying false alarms.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why is accuracy a misleading metric for rare events like spotting fraud?

options.text: A model can get high accuracy by always guessing the majority 'no fraud' class
Fraud is impossible to predict with linear models
Accuracy requires too much memory to compute on large datasets

exercises.explain: Correct! On highly imbalanced data, guessing the majority class guarantees high accuracy while catching nothing.

cards.title: The overzealous spam blocker
cards.body: Have you ever missed an important email because it got sent to your spam folder? A model that flags important mail as spam is making a costly mistake.

cards.title: What is precision?
cards.body: Precision is the proportion of positive predictions that are actually correct. It asks: Out of all the times the model said 'Yes', how many times was it right?

cards.title: Guilty until proven innocent?
cards.body: Think of a judge. High precision means the judge only convicts people when they are absolutely sure of guilt, minimizing the risk of sending an innocent person to prison.

cards.title: Spam filtering
cards.body: If a spam filter flags 10 emails as spam, and 9 of them are actually spam but 1 is an important work email, the filter's precision is 9 out of 10, or 90%.

cards.title: Myth: Precision is the only metric that matters
cards.body: False. A model could achieve 100% precision by making only one positive prediction all day that it is absolutely sure of. But it might miss hundreds of other positive cases!

cards.title: The cost of low precision
cards.body: Why precision matters in spam filtering:

left.text:
Only flags real spam.
No important emails are lost.

right.text:
Flags normal emails too.
You miss important messages.

exercises.prompt: If a model predicts 20 transactions are fraudulent, but only 15 of them actually are, what is the model's precision?
exercises.explain: Precision is positive correct guesses divided by all positive guesses: 15 / 20 = 75%.

exercises.prompt: True or false: High precision means the model makes very few false alarms.
exercises.explain: True. High precision means that when the model says 'positive', it is highly likely to be correct, which keeps false alarms low.

exercises.sentence: Precision measures correct positive predictions out of all ___ positive predictions.
exercises.explain: Precision is calculated out of all predictions the model flagged as positive, regardless of whether they were correct.

exercises.prompt: Which of these scenarios describes a model with 100% precision?

options.text: A model flags exactly 1 email as spam, and it is spam. It ignores 50 other spam emails.
A model flags 100 emails as spam, and 50 are spam while 50 are real emails.
A model flags zero emails as spam.

exercises.explain: Every single positive prediction was correct (1 out of 1), so precision is 100% even though it missed many others.

exercises.prompt: A streaming service wants to recommend movies you will love. If they recommend a movie you dislike, it ruins the experience. Should they optimize for precision?

options.text: Yes, because they want to ensure that when they make a recommendation, it is highly likely to be correct.
No, they should recommend every movie in the catalog to be safe.
No, because precision only applies to classification of spam or health data.
Yes, because precision guarantees they will recommend every single movie you could possibly like.

exercises.explain: Exactly! High precision ensures recommendations are highly accurate, avoiding bad suggestions that annoy the user. 🎉
